senses.definition: Gradually increases over time, especially money, interest, or benefits.
examples: Interest accrues on this account at a fixed rate each year.
Additional vacation days accrue to employees who stay with the company longer.
The financial benefits of the project will accrue over the next decade.

senses.definition: Gradually increasing over time, especially money, interest, or benefits.
examples: The debt is accruing faster than they can pay it off.
He checked how much interest was accruing in his savings account.
Unpaid fees had been accruing for months.

senses.definition: Written abbreviation for account or accountant, used especially in business writing.
examples: Please transfer the funds to my savings acct immediately.
Our company has opened a new acct with the bank.
She works as a senior acct in a large firm.

senses.definition: Written abbreviation for accounts, used especially in business writing.
examples: The manager reviewed all company accts before the meeting.
Please send the updated accts to our auditor.
Sales and expense accts must be checked every month.

senses.definition: The act or posture of reclining on a couch while eating, especially in ancient times.
examples: In Roman culture, accubation at meals showed high social status.
The painting depicts the custom of accubation during a feast.
Accubation was common among wealthy families in classical societies.

senses.definition: Plural of accubation: acts or instances of reclining on a couch while eating, especially in ancient times.
examples: The author describes different forms of accubations in Roman banquets.
Archaeologists have found images of accubations on ancient pottery.

senses.definition: Couches or reclining seats used for dining in ancient times.
examples: The museum displays several reconstructed accubita from Roman villas.
Guests lay on accubita arranged around the table.

senses.definition: A single couch or reclining seat used for dining in ancient times.
examples: Each accubitum could hold several diners.
The stone base of an accubitum was found in the ruins.

senses.definition: The posture or state of reclining on a dining couch in ancient times.
examples: Accubitus was a normal part of elite Roman banquets.
The sculpture captures the relaxed accubitus of the figures.

senses.definition: A welcome or reception, especially how someone is received by others.
examples: Her book received a warm accueil from critics.
The proposal met with a mixed accueil in the community.
The film's poor accueil surprised the director.

senses.definition: Relating to culture or to the process of cultural change.
examples: The study focuses on accultural influences in migrant communities.
Accultural factors can affect how students adapt to a new school.
They examined accultural differences between generations.

senses.definition: To change or adapt when coming into contact with a different culture.
examples: Immigrants often acculturate quickly to the local lifestyle.
Second-generation children tend to acculturate more easily than their parents.
The program helps newcomers acculturate to the school environment.

senses.definition: Having adapted to a different culture or adopted many of its customs.
examples: They are highly acculturated and speak the local language fluently.
Acculturated youths may feel distant from their parents' traditions.
The more acculturated group showed different health behaviors.

senses.definition: Changes or adapts to a different culture or its customs.
examples: The community slowly acculturates to its new surroundings.
Each group acculturates at its own pace.
The study tracks how quickly each family acculturates.

senses.definition: Changing or adapting to a different culture or its customs over time.
examples: The children are still acculturating to their new school system.
Acculturating to a new country can be stressful.
Families acculturating at different speeds may face conflict.

senses.definition: The process of cultural and psychological change that happens when groups or individuals from different cultures come into continuous contact.
examples: The study examines the acculturation of migrants in urban areas.
Acculturation can affect language use and identity.
Health outcomes are often linked to the level of acculturation.

senses.definition: Relating to the process of acculturation or cultural change.
examples: The research explores acculturational differences among age groups.
Acculturational factors influence mental health outcomes.
They developed a scale to measure acculturational stress.

senses.definition: A researcher or specialist who studies acculturation and related cultural changes.
examples: As an acculturationist, she focuses on immigrant youth.
Acculturationists disagree on how to measure cultural adaptation.

senses.definition: Plural of acculturation: different instances or types of cultural change caused by contact between cultures.
examples: The book compares various acculturations across regions.
Different acculturations can lead to unique community identities.

senses.definition: Connected with acculturation or the effects of adapting to a new culture.
examples: Acculturative stress can lead to anxiety and depression.
The paper describes acculturative changes among refugees.
They investigated acculturative influences on family relationships.

senses.definition: To cause a person or group to adopt the culture or customs of another group.
examples: Colonial policies often tried to acculturize local populations.
Schools were used to acculturize children into the dominant culture.
Attempts to acculturize minorities have sometimes harmed traditional practices.

senses.definition: Having been made to adopt or strongly influenced by another culture.
examples: The acculturized community lost many of its original customs.
Acculturized groups may feel caught between two worlds.
He grew up in a highly acculturized environment.

senses.definition: Making someone or a group adopt the culture or customs of another group over time.
examples: Policies aimed at acculturizing indigenous children had lasting effects.
Schools played a key role in acculturizing new arrivals.
Acculturizing one group to another can create tension.

senses.definition: Informal written form of accumulate, meaning to collect or build up over time.
examples: Dust tends to accum in the corners.
Data will accum over several months of testing.
Small errors can accum and cause big problems.

senses.definition: To lie down or recline, especially at a table, in an old or literary style.
examples: They would accumb on couches during the feast.
Guests accumbed beside the low table in the painting.

senses.definition: Plural of accumbency: states or periods of lying down or reclining, especially at a table.
examples: The artist shows several leisurely accumbencies around the table.
Different accumbencies are depicted in the mural.

senses.definition: The state or posture of lying down or reclining, especially at a table.
examples: Accumbency was a sign of comfort and status in some ancient cultures.
The sculpture captures a moment of relaxed accumbency.

senses.definition: Lying down or reclining, especially while eating.
examples: The figures are shown in an accumbent pose around the table.
An accumbent position at meals was common among Roman elites.

senses.definition: To weigh down or burden, especially in an old-fashioned way.
examples: He did not wish to accumber his friends with his troubles.
The travelers were accumbered by heavy luggage.

senses.definition: Able to be collected or built up over time.
examples: These small savings are easily accumulable over several years.
The study focuses on accumulable environmental risks.

senses.definition: To collect or build up something over time.
examples: He managed to accumulate enough money to buy a house.
Dust can accumulate quickly if you do not clean regularly.
Over the years, she accumulated a lot of experience in the field.

senses.definition: Gradually collected or built up over time.
examples: They used their accumulated savings to start a business.
The report shows the accumulated costs of the project.
Her accumulated knowledge made her a respected expert.

senses.definition: Collected or built up something over time.
examples: He accumulated a large amount of data for his study.
The company had accumulated significant debts.
Over the years, they accumulated many rare books.

senses.definition: Collects or builds up something over time.
examples: Snow accumulates on the mountaintop in winter.
Interest accumulates on the loan each month.
Plastic waste accumulates in the oceans.

senses.definition: Collecting or building up something over time.
examples: They are accumulating funds for their new project.
Problems had been accumulating for years.
The team is accumulating data for the experiment.

senses.definition: A gradual increase or collection of things over time.
examples: The accumulation of dust can damage electronic equipment.
There has been a steady accumulation of evidence against him.
We studied the accumulation of wealth in modern economies.

senses.definition: Things that have collected or built up over time.
examples: Large accumulations of snow blocked the roads.
These fat accumulations can increase health risks.
The report covers several accumulations of data.

senses.definition: Nonstandard or shortened form of accumulative, meaning tending to collect or build up.
examples: The text seems to use an older form, 'accumulativ,' for 'accumulative'.
He wrote of the 'accumulativ power' of small actions.

senses.definition: Increasing or growing by collecting more of something over time.
examples: The drug has an accumulative effect on the body.
Learning is an accumulative process built on past knowledge.
Their accumulative experience helped them solve the problem quickly.

senses.definition: In a way that increases by adding more over time.
examples: The changes affect the company accumulatively rather than all at once.
The risks grow accumulatively with each extra dose.
Savings grow accumulatively as you add more each month.

senses.definition: The quality of tending to collect or build up things, especially money or possessions.
examples: His strong accumulativeness led him to focus on saving.
The novel criticizes the accumulativeness of modern society.
Accumulativeness can sometimes conflict with generosity.

senses.definition: Plural of accumulativeness: different types or instances of a tendency to collect or build up things.
examples: The book explores various accumulativenesses in consumer behavior.
Different cultural accumulativenesses affect saving habits.

senses.definition: Someone or something that collects or builds up things over time.
examples: He is a careful accumulator of rare books.
The device acts as an energy accumulator in the system.
As an information accumulator, the database stores years of data.

senses.definition: People or things that collect or build up things over time.
examples: Some investors are long-term accumulators of stock.
The system uses several accumulators to store energy.
These databases are powerful accumulators of personal information.

senses.definition: Nonstandard or obsolete spelling of occupy, meaning to live in, fill, or use a place or space.
examples: An old text used the form 'accupy' instead of 'occupy'.
They would accupy the land through force.

senses.definition: Obsolete spelling variant related to occur, meaning to happen or appear.
examples: In older documents, 'accur' may appear where we now write 'occur'.
Such events did not often accur in that region.

senses.definition: Plural of accuracy: the levels or degrees of how correct or exact something is.
examples: Different devices showed different accuracies in measurement.
We compared the accuracies of several prediction models.
Higher accuracies are needed for medical tests.

senses.definition: The quality of being correct, exact, or free from mistakes.
examples: The accuracy of the results was checked by another lab.
Good spelling and accuracy are important in formal writing.
This device has a high level of accuracy.

senses.definition: Correct and exact, with no mistakes.
examples: The figures in the report are accurate.
She gave an accurate description of the suspect.
It is difficult to make an accurate prediction.

senses.definition: In a way that is correct and exact, with no mistakes.
examples: The machine can accurately measure very small changes.
It is hard to accurately predict the result.
She accurately reported what had happened.

senses.definition: The state or quality of being accurate.
examples: He checked the accurateness of the data several times.
The accurateness of her observations impressed the researchers.
Accurateness is vital in scientific experiments.
